items.q: Is the proton a fundamental particle?
items.correct: No — it is made of two up quarks and one down quark; the electron, by contrast, is fundamental.
items.misconception: Yes — protons cannot be broken down any further.
items.random_wrong: No — protons are made of electrons.
items.why: Students learn protons as 'building blocks' at GCSE and assume they are elementary rather than composite.

items.q: In beta-minus decay, where does the emitted electron come from?
items.correct: A neutron in the nucleus changes into a proton, creating an electron and an antineutrino.
items.misconception: It is one of the atom's orbital electrons knocked out of its shell.
items.random_wrong: It is a proton that has lost its charge.
items.why: Students assume the beta particle must be an existing orbital electron, not a particle created in the decay itself.

items.q: Are X-rays and gamma rays fundamentally different kinds of radiation?
items.correct: No — both are high-energy electromagnetic radiation; they differ mainly in origin (atomic/tube processes versus the nucleus).
items.misconception: Yes — gamma rays are particles while X-rays are waves.
items.random_wrong: Yes — X-rays are a form of sound wave.
items.why: The different names suggest different physics, but the distinction is the source, not the nature of the radiation.

items.q: A proton consists of which quarks?
items.why: Correct answer: uud. “udd” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In β⁻ decay, the exchange particle is:
items.why: Correct answer: W⁻ boson. “Photon” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
